sentences.text: Well, everything went back to the normal.
I don't know exactly what did they do with the-- with the parcels which they took away from the people.
I know that we-- I mean, people in Theresienstadt, thought that now something will happen, now the world will know that the Jews are here, and they are not really free.
And something might be for better.
But nothing happened.
I didn't hear anything about it anymore.
I think it must have been in summer, because around that time it was very hot at night.

And I remember one occasion when we were very hot in the place where we stayed-- in the hospital-- and everybody took a bed out on the yard in the-- there was a big yard-- and we slept outside on under the stars.
And that was wonderful.
It was adventure for us.
Don't forget we were young.
I was then turning 19-- not yet-- I was 18 when I came there.
I was living one year in Theresienstadt.
I have seen-- later, I have been working also-- not only in children's part, also at adults.
And I've seen my first person who was dead.
And unfortunately for me, it was a great shock because that lady, her name was like my mother's name-- Teresa.
It was dreadful for me, because at that time I visualize-- I don't know where my mother is.
Is she alive?
Is she dead?
Here was a person.
It was dreadful.
But being young and having support of my sister and support of the other sisters who were working there, and the doctors were very good.
Doctors were looking after the children as much as they could.
And occasionally seeing my husband-- I recovered from this shock, and we lived as well as we could.
It was called [NON-ENGLISH], it was Vrchlabi.
And that was the part where the hospital was.
We didn't go out much.
As I said, we have been separated.
But we had very good teachers.
There was a nurse-- I remember her.
She was called Tante, Auntie, Auntie, Auntie Betta-- I can't remember the name, but we loved her.
And she was teaching us what to do in nursing.
And she was wonderful.
She was like a mother to us.
Her husband was a doctor.
And he was working there just as well.
They came from Vienna, I think, or from Germany.
We spoke German with those people there.
We had to speak German in Czechoslovakia already.
Because when Hitler occupied and Germany occupied Czechoslovakia, many-- we had to speak in German.
And--
Well, we didn't-- we didn't have to speak German.
But we-- if we wanted to be in-- in line, and read orders and so on, it was translated from Czech to German.
We started to.
We were taught German at school as a second language.
So and our auntie was from Vienna.
And she-- we were teaching her Czech, and she was speaking to us in German sometimes.
We were talking about Theresienstadt.

I remember that then in December, we were-- Annetta-- sorry-- my husband as a ghetto werker, ghetto werkman-- as a punishment, the ghetto werkmen-- ghetto werkmen, the police people, for-- for punishment for some-- something-- somebody was not obeying orders or what.
The group of the people was supposed to go to be sent out on transport.
And my husband was in that transport.
And therefore, I was in that transport.
That was in-- September-- October-- October, '43.
And my sister was not in that transport.
But being in the hospital and being friendly with the doctors and with the nurses, my husband had falsified that he has got-- he is sick.
He had scarlet-- he had diphtheria.
That was said, he's had diphtheria.
There was a swab taken from his throat.
And in the laboratories, it was exchanged for somebody who did have diphtheria.
And therefore, he was taken out of the transport.
Because people who were infectious disease, sick, they were not sent on the transport-- the rules.
So he was staying in the hospital.
And therefore, I and Annetta, we were staying there.
Annetta was not in the transport.
But I, as his wife, was there, too.
But then in December, it came to it that Annetta was chosen for the transport again.
Or was it Egon and I?
I am not sure.
I think it was Egon and I.
That's right-- Egon and I, we were sent.
We were chosen to go to-- in transport to east.
And Annetta volunteered.
And this time, she was allowed.
Because I think it must have been the selections they made somehow through the registrations, through the Judenrat, the Jewish administration.
So somehow, she was allowed to come with us.
So Egon, Annetta, and I, we were sent to Auschwitz.
No, no.
We were not.
But there was-- there were news coming through.
And people were whispering.
It was like a grape line, on the grape line.
And we always hoped that now that is the news arbeiter.
But we didn't have newspapers.
And we didn't have [INAUDIBLE], we didn't have telephones.
We have been a very, very primitive way of living.
It was very primitive.
Well, whatever we brought from Czechoslovakia we still possessed, that was not taken away from us.
We have taken that.
We might have been given by somebody something extra, a little suitcase.
There was always a lot of chaos.
And I remember that somebody left suitcase-- there was suitcase left behind.
And it was not-- it didn't belong to anybody, so it was distributed between people.
And I think I have got a-- a alarm clock or something.
But we didn't have it later, anyway.
We took our suitcases.
We took some food.
And we were sent in a-- a cattle truck from Theresienstadt.
The station was built onto the Theresienstadt proper.
So we didn't have to go into the township.
And we were sent to Auschwitz with other people.
That was the first realization that we have been treated not like human being.
We were treated like animals.
Because we have been forced to go into cattle truck.
We have been there-- it was overcrowded.
There was no way to lie down or sit.
We have-- I can't remember how did we really manage to be in.
I think we have been sitting.
There was no light-- it was dark.
There was that small little window, which was barbed.
Or I don't know how it was exactly.
But there was no light, really.
It was barbed-- closed from the outside.
Nobody could open it from inside.
And there was one bucket which was used as a toilet for all the people in.
And I was the very unfortunate one who had a running stomach-- excitement, I don't know.
And I had to use it.
And that was the worst thing I could remember in my life, the shock that I had to use a toilet seat-- or without a seat, bucket, as a toilet in front of strangers.
When the toilet was-- when the bucket was full during the journey, it was just thrown through the window.
Somebody has to lift it up through the bars to throw it out.
And that probably must ended on the windows of the other trucks.
I don't know, but it was dreadful.
And that was-- that was-- I don't know how long the journey took.
And I don't know what we did.
We just-- we just sat there and waited.
We didn't even talk.
I don't how long it took.
Then we arrived in Auschwitz.
What food was--
We had some food with us.
I think that we might have been eating on the way some bread, which we had.
But we haven't been given anything.
Whoever had something with-- with them might have been eating it.
And I really can't recollect how long it took.
For us, it was eternity.
And when we came to Auschwitz, we had to spill out of the truck.
It was high.
There was maybe some timber there that we-- or we jumped down.
I'm not sure.
It was darkness.
It was cold.
We were frightened.
There were SS guards with dogs.
There were people-- we didn't know who they are-- in stripe uniforms who were shouting at us.
They were the kapos.
You might have heart about them later.
They were the ones who were guarding-- were the helpers.
They had to do it.
That was their job.
They were kept.
They were treated probably better, but they didn't treat the newcomers very well.
They came to us, and they told us if you have something, watch or some valuables, give it to me.
You won't need it.
It will be taken away from you, but I can help you somehow later.
Yes.
I'm sure that they did.
But I didn't see anybody, because I was-- I was jumping out and not looking back.
But there were people who died during the journey.
But they  were just thrown out of the truck then.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we came on the platform, we were told-- it was shouted.
We were shouted at.
Men and women had to be separated.
I didn't even have a chance to say goodbye to my husband, because he was sent to one side and I and my sister to other side-- women with children, older people to one side.
We have been-- as a matter of fact, we were sent to a Familienlager-- family lager-- family lager, which was called B II B-- It was Birkenau.
And we have been sent there, because the previous transport was sent there, too.
I don't know what the reason was that the people from previous transport were kept there before we came.
But after we came, the people from the first transport, which we escaped then, were sent to gas chamber.
Or some of them were sent to work.
I'm not sure.
We met only a couple of people who were saved from the previous transport.
Because we were in the Familienlager.
Later, Annetta and I, we were selected as twins.
Mengele, Hauptsturmfuhrer Mengele, was a doctor in Auschwitz, whose privilege was to use human beings as Guinea pigs for experiments.
And from every transport, there was selection done for twins.
Dwarfs were selected, there were dwarfs coming also.
And there were gypsies coming.
Gypsies had their own block.
There was a lager which was called Zigeunerlager-- Gypsy camp.
And they have been families kept together for some time.
And we, we're not kept together.
We have been separated, men and women, but not for long.
I don't know what questions would you like to ask.
Maybe I'm running too much.
Well, there were the SS men who made the selection.
And the helpers were the kapos or the gendarmes.
The selections were made by Dr.
Mengele, or people like him who have been selecting, with flick of the finger to right or left, to gas chamber or to live a little bit longer to be useful as a slave labor.
The women and children and the older ones, the sick people who looked sick, were sent to one side and then were sent to gas chambers.
I have been all the time with Annetta.
We had been always together, not because we wanted to be, but we have been selected to be together then as twins.
That was at the time when we were selected as twins to be kept aside, not to be sent to work somewhere else, or whatever.
That's when we met him.
He was a good-looking man.
He was nonchalantly standing there, you know, flicking his finger to right or left to selections, and then telling us, you are twins?
Speaking German-- are you identical twins?
Good, good.
And then we were taken to the other side.
But at that time, we actually didn't know that we are in Auschwitz.
We didn't know where we are.
When we arrived in Auschwitz, it was a-- like a different planet for us.
We didn't know where we are.
And being sended in, at the original-- when we came at the time, we were sent to big building, big hall, where we had to be stripped naked.
And then we have been given some other clothes.
Our clothes was taken away.
We haven't been keeping anything from our clothes.
And then we were sent to showers.
But at that time, we didn't know really that there are some other buildings which have like showers and they are gas.
At the time, we have been sent to shower.
And we showered there.
We have been given soap, which was something disinfectant.
We haven't been cut-- our hair was not cut.
Because we were sent to the Familienlager.
And our hair was not cut, only on the--
We were given tattooed numbers, yes.
I better show you--
This tattoo number.
We have been tattooed numbers.
My number is 72,919.
And you might see the two numbers, the first numbers are lighter than the darker, the other ones.
That was because a child was given by mistake 72,515-- and I had the same number originally that was tattooed to me.
And they wanted to-- they made a mistake.
And they wanted to tattoo the child's number again to correct it.
So I volunteered.
At they tattooed my number over it.
It was originally 515.
But now it is 72,919.
Because- that is why my number is stronger.
My sister's number was a bit different, because we have been tattooed by alphabetical order.
So her number was not exactly the same.
And my sister had had number removed when she came here to Australia.
She didn't want it.
But my children said there is no need for you to remove it.
You don't need to be ashamed for it.
The others ought to be ashamed for giving it to you.
What happened next?
Then I didn't see my husband then for some time, even though that he was in the same camp.
We have been given a place on a bunk.
I really can't remember very much the details.
You have to realize that I tried so much to forget, that I tried to rub it out of mind.
And then now when I start back, you know, occasionally a glimpse come.
And sometimes I didn't even think that it is possibility that it was happening.
And I wouldn't believe it myself.
What happened then?
My husband has been given some kind of job delousing.
And he was going from block to block cleaning the clothes.
Or I really don't know.
I have met him in the washroom.
We had the washroom, and I met him.
And he was very, very depressed young man.
He was not the same personality.
And he said, we are not going to survive this.
And couldn't do anything about it.
I just try to tell him I am optimistic.
It won't last-- last long.
And we will stay together.
But it was probably the last time I have seen him.
And my sister I, we were-- have been put on a block during the day where there were sick people.
And it was like a krankenbau-- you would call it a sick bay.
And there was not much comfort for the people.
And we didn't have much chance to help them.
We just had to feed them with the soup what they were given or something.
We had-- I don't know even if we had bed pans.
I know that we had buckets at the back of the building.
And we had to empty the-- whatever we used, like pots for them, into it and then carry it out into the latrines.
I don't know if you know what latrines mean.
Latrines were the toilets.
It was wooden planks higher up built on a higher stand.
And there were about 10 next to each other.
The planks were-- in the planks were buckets.
And the buckets were used like toilets.
And somebody was in charge.
And sometime, there was piece of paper, maybe, that they said they gave us when we went there.
But I can't remember very well how did we use, how did we manage without the toilet paper?
It was newspaper probably.
The buckets were to be used.
And you had to sit on the plank.
And the guard was supposed to-- prisoners-- were supposed to watch that you don't dirty it.
And it was very hard to use the latrines.
And in this sick bay, there were people-- I didn't know at that time, but probably the twins that we have been-- you know, as twins.
They might have been experimented on.
And we didn't really know what was done to them.
I remember that was on a couple girls from Holland.
We couldn't speak to them.
They spoke only Dutch.
And one of them was operated on.
I really can't remember what it was.
And one of them died.
And when we had to take the body down, it was dreadful.
She had sores.
And there were even worms already, you know.
It was dreadful.
And what other thing was?
There was one young girl.
I knew she was operated on.
She has-- she was pregnant.
She was a young married woman who was pregnant when she came to Auschwitz, to Mengele's care.
And she has been on-- in six months aborted.
They operated on her to abort her to know if a woman can survive six months abortion.
She did survive.
But she was sent to gas chamber afterwards, anyway.
There was time when Annetta and I, we had to carry corpses.
Every time, every day, there were people dying.
And the corpses were lying behind the barracks heaped up.
They were most of them, most of the time naked.
Because the clothing that the dead people had was taken away by other prisoners in the survivor urge.
I mean, the dead ones didn't need it anymore.
So if somebody had still something on that you could use, you could be warmer.
You could exchange for your dirty or torn garment, it was taken off.
And Annetta and I, we had to-- I remember close-- very, very clearly one occasion-- there were many more, but one occasion.
It was dark, and we hit to be there with the corpses, which were a big pile.
And we had to take one the hands, one the arms, one the legs and heap them up on the lorry.
On the lorry, somebody else was standing, piling them up higher, higher.
The sight of those corpses was dreadful.
Because not only they were skeletons and sickly looking, we couldn't see if somebody was there whom we knew.
Because you didn't recognize even your own friends.
Some of them have even pus, you know, oozing.
Bandages-- if there were bandages, were only paper bandages, crepe bandage, was hanging down.
I remember one corpse had half of the face eaten up by rat.
Work like that had to be done.
And we couldn't shrink from it.
We had to do it.
And after the work, when the lorry took the corpses to the crematoria to be burned, we came to the barrack.
And we didn't have any change of clothing, no overalls.
We washed our hands.
There was not a shower, nowhere for the baths.
Every time when I have a hot shower with nice soap, I remember this location that we had to wash our hands with ice cold water.
Instead of soap, we had some kind of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] chemical which is used now in swimming pools, I think, something very strong.
We washed with that.
And then we have been given a reward.
The reward of extra portion of soup or maybe extra portion of bread.
Now you don't think that you could eat after work like that, but we did.
We had to, because we wanted to survive.
It was a urge to survive from minute to minute, from day to day.
And we did it.
And we have been more or less like robots doing this kind of work, looking after the dying.
We have seen so many dying and handled so many dead ones, I can't count anymore.
For me, at that time, it was just a work which we had to do.
And we had to push our emotions back.
It was not a question of thinking-- this must have been a girl who might have looked nice, nothing like that.
We just couldn't think.
We just had to be robots.
That might have been a way of surviving.
Because if we would sit down and cried, we would have collapsed.
That might have been the way that we could do it.
Beg your pardon?
A long, long building, which was originally stable.
There were horses kept.
And then there were bunks made like square, like a bed, which I don't know if-- you know what bunks are.
There's three layers bunks.
And people were lying on bottom bunks, middle, and top.
And there were no mattresses.
I don't know if we had some kind of a straw or something.
I really don't-- I think it was just plain timber that the we-- wood that we were lying on.
And in the middle of the whole building, or whole the barrack, there was like a chimney.
There was a-- a-- it went through.
And there was-- you could sit on it.
It was cement, kind of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like a table or whatever, which went through the whole building.
And on one side, there was a fire going like a oven or stove where eventually, you might have warmed up something if you have stolen-- if you were lucky enough to get couple of potatoes or something.
Or you have your bread.
And you might have been able to toast it.
I don't know, really.
I can't remember exactly.
But the whole building was just the middle where there was this-- we called it chimney-- come in, come in, like a stove.
I don't really know, can't describe it-- and the bunks.
And in the front of it, there was a separated little room, which was for the blockalteste-- that was the prisoner who was in charge of the block.
She was the one who got-- who made sure that everything was in order, it was clean as much as it was possible, that people were coming at 4 o'clock in the morning for there was wake-up.
To come out in the yard to be counted, we had to stand for hours and hours to counted in rows.
If you were sick, and you couldn't stand there, and you were just falling down, somebody helped you to stand up.
There were long hours of counting when the SS men came-- and the kapos, and the blockalteste, everybody was in charge, very important, counting.
And we had to stand if it was raining or not.
And if somebody was missing, that she was sick and she was still lying on the bunk and couldn't get down, or somebody went to the latrine and didn't come out in time, we had to stand there and wait.
Good.
